summary: The post highlights an overlooked hygiene gap during the COVID-19 pandemic: while people focus on handwashing, they ignore that their constantly-touched phones accumulate and redistribute germs. O'Neil walks through a typical daily scenario showing how washing hands becomes futile when we immediately pick up a dirty phone. She notes the irony of discussing pandemic hygiene while touching her face and phone, and acknowledges that solving this problem will require new cleaning procedures that most people haven't considered.
key_points: Handwashing is undermined when we immediately touch contaminated phones afterward
Phones are constantly touched throughout the day, accumulating germs from every surface we contact
The irony of discussing hygiene practices while engaging in the very behaviors that spread germs
COVID-19 can survive on phone surfaces for extended periods, making this a real transmission risk
Developing effective phone-cleaning habits will require careful attention to avoid recontamination
money_quotes: I'm on the phone talking about this, while copiously touching my face and my dirty phone.
It occurred to me that washing our hands won't be all that helpful if our phones are dirty, which they of course will be because we cannot stop using our phones even for a minute.
Go on subway, touch things, read phone, get to destination, touch things, put down phone, go to the bathroom, wash hands carefully, pick up phone again.
The only germs we're not getting on our phone is actual bathroom germs, and that's only if you don't bring your phone with you to the bathroom, which let's face it people do in general.
I'm dizzy with the procedure we will have to follow assuming we have wipes that actually clean our phones and our hands.
key_insight: Our phones are the weak link in pandemic hygiene because they constantly touch our hands and faces, making handwashing alone insufficient.
